questions.question: What is the single most important distinction defining what it means to facilitate?
options: The facilitator owns the HOW (process); the group owns the WHAT (content)
The facilitator owns the WHAT (content); the group owns the HOW (process)
The facilitator decides the outcome and the group simply executes it
The facilitator and the group jointly own both process and content equally
questions.explanation: Facilitation means owning the process and staying neutral on content so the group owns its own decision. Owning the WHAT would be leading, not facilitating; blurring the line makes the group stop owning the outcome.

questions.question: By the neutrality test, if participants could guess your opinion on the decision from how you ran the session, you were leading rather than facilitating.
questions.explanation: The neutrality test is exactly this: if people couldn't guess your opinion from how you ran it, you facilitated; if they could, you leaked a content opinion and were leading.

questions.question: Per the 2020 Scrum Guide, the Daily Scrum is best described as:
options: A 15-minute Developer-owned planning event to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal
A 15-minute status report the Developers give to the Scrum Master each day
A 30-minute event the Product Owner runs to reassign the day's tasks
An optional event where Developers answer the three mandatory questions
questions.explanation: The Daily is a 15-minute, Developer-owned planning event focused on the Sprint Goal and re-planning the next 24 hours. It is not a status report, the PO does not run it, and the 2020 Guide dropped (did not mandate) the three questions.

questions.question: How many events does the 2020 Scrum Guide define, and how is Backlog Refinement classified?
options: Five events (the Sprint contains the other four); Backlog Refinement is an ongoing activity, not an event
Four events; Backlog Refinement is counted as the fifth Scrum event
Five events, with Backlog Refinement counted as one of the five
Six events, including both Backlog Refinement and the Sprint itself
questions.explanation: There are five events: the Sprint as the container plus Planning, Daily, Review, and Retrospective. Backlog Refinement is an ongoing activity and must not be called a Scrum event.

questions.question: Sprint Planning answers Why/What/How and the team walks out with which two outputs?
options: A Sprint Goal and a Sprint Backlog
A Sprint Goal and a revised Product Backlog
An Increment and a Definition of Done
A velocity number and a signed-off commitment
questions.explanation: Planning produces the Sprint Goal (the Why) and the Sprint Backlog (selected items plus enough plan). The revised Product Backlog is a Sprint Review output, and the Increment is built during the Sprint.

questions.question: An event that ends without changing anything is 'a status meeting, not an empirical loop.' What is the underlying reasoning?
options: Adaptation is the proof that inspection actually happened
Every event has a fixed timebox that must be fully consumed
Inspection can only occur if external stakeholders are present
Transparency requires that no plan ever changes mid-Sprint
questions.explanation: Scrum events are inspect-and-adapt loops; a visible adaptation (to the plan, backlog, or way of working) is the evidence that inspection occurred. The other options misstate timeboxes, attendance, and the purpose of transparency.

questions.question: A Daily Scrum has become a round of reports aimed at the Scrum Master. Which move most directly fixes the dynamic?
options: Walk the board right-to-left and step physically back so Developers talk to each other
Reinstate the three mandatory questions so everyone is given a turn
Have the Product Owner run the event to keep the team on track
Extend the timebox to 30 minutes so everyone can finish reporting
questions.explanation: The fix is to reframe from report to re-plan: walk the board (not the people) and step back so the test is that your absence wouldn't break it. Reinstating the questions, PO ownership, and extending the timebox all reinforce the status-report smell.

questions.question: Which framing of the Sprint Review is correct, and what is the most common failure mode?
options: A working session to inspect the Increment with stakeholders and adapt the Product Backlog; the common failure is demo theater with no backlog changes
A sign-off gate where stakeholders formally accept the Sprint; the common failure is the team forgetting to ask for approval
A private team-only demo of the build; the common failure is inviting too many outside stakeholders into the room
The event where the team inspects how it worked; the common failure is skipping the Prime Directive at the start
questions.explanation: The Review inspects the Increment with stakeholders and adapts the Product Backlog; demo theater (a polished presentation with no real software and no backlog changes) is inspection without adaptation. Sign-off framing and team-only demos are themselves antipatterns; inspecting how we worked is the Retrospective.

questions.question: In the Derby and Larsen five-step arc, why is Gather Data sequenced before Generate Insights?
options: Jumping to insights or solutions before establishing shared facts produces opinion fights instead of analysis
Generate Insights is optional and only ever run when metrics are available
Gathering data is the only phase that actually requires psychological safety
Insights must always be voted on before any data can be collected
questions.explanation: Facts first, then meaning, then action: establishing a shared factual picture prevents the opinion fights that come from leaping to root cause or solutions prematurely. The other options misstate the phases and the role of safety and voting.

questions.question: Why is consent called the 'agile sweet spot' compared with consensus and voting?
options: It asks 'does anyone object (safe to try)?', so it is faster than consensus and stickier than voting because dissent is heard
It requires unanimous active agreement, which gives it the single highest possible buy-in of the three
It is the fastest of the three rules because the majority preference simply wins outright
It eliminates dissent entirely by requiring every objection to be fully resolved before proceeding
questions.explanation: Consent proceeds unless there is a reasoned, safe-to-try objection: faster than consensus (which seeks agreement and can deadlock on a holdout) and producing more buy-in than voting (which leaves a losing minority). Requiring unanimous agreement describes consensus, and 'majority wins' describes voting.

questions.question: The meta-move for nearly every group dysfunction is to 'change the structure, not the person.' Why is the structural fix preferred over calling someone out?
options: A structural fix (silent writing, round-robin, timebox) corrects behavior without singling anyone out and triggering defensiveness
Calling someone out is faster and more reliable, but it happens to violate the agreed timebox
Structural fixes let the facilitator quietly decide the content on the group's behalf
Naming the person publicly is the only reliable way to enforce working agreements
questions.explanation: Structural changes make airtime structurally equal and correct behavior without naming a person, avoiding the defensiveness that scolding provokes. Structural fixes do not let the facilitator decide content, and working agreements are group-authored, not imposed by public call-outs.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly describes the Liberating Structure '1-2-4-All', and what problem is it the named antidote to?
options: Think alone, pair up, join into a four, then share to the whole group; antidote to both the dominator and the silent room
Whole group brainstorm, then split into fours, then pairs, then individuals; antidote to over-structuring a meeting
One facilitator, two scribes, four voters, and all the rest observers; antidote to unclear group decision rules
Alone for one minute, two minutes paired, four minutes in fours, all day; antidote to overly short timeboxes
questions.explanation: 1-2-4-All funnels from silent individual thinking through pairs and foursomes to the whole group, so ideas are pressure-tested and everyone has already spoken. It is the structure to name for getting a quiet group talking while also containing the dominator.

questions.question: Read as a consent gradient, a participant showing two fingers on a fist-of-five should be interpreted as:
options: Reservations to discuss — a conversation, not a veto
A hard block that stops the proposal outright
Full support that can be recorded as a yes
An abstention that is ignored in the tally
questions.explanation: On the gradient, 0 = block, 1 = serious concerns, 2 = reservations to discuss, 3 = will go along, 4 = support, 5 = champion. Anything below 3 is a conversation to surface, not a veto; only 0 is a block, so two fingers is not a hard stop, a yes, or an ignorable abstention.

questions.question: In the double-diamond, what is the facilitator's correct response to the groan zone, and why?
options: Hold it rather than escape early, because premature convergence produces shallow decisions
Escape it as fast as possible, because the discomfort signals the group has lost focus
Skip the first diamond entirely so the group can converge on solutions much sooner
Converge during divergence so weak ideas are killed before they spread further
questions.explanation: The groan zone is the normal messy middle; deciding early just to escape discomfort yields shallow decisions, so the facilitator names and holds it. Skipping the first diamond solves the wrong problem, and converging during divergence collapses the diamond and loses the good-idea tail.

questions.question: Disagreeing with the content of a decision the group is converging on is, by itself, legitimate grounds for a facilitator to intervene.
questions.explanation: Intervene only when process, safety, timebox, or participation is at risk — never merely because you disagree with the content. Intervening on a content opinion is leading, not facilitating.

questions.question: A team keeps surfacing the same complaints every retro with nothing changing ('Groundhog Day'). Which of the following directly address the root cause? (Select all that apply.)
options: Converge to one owned, dated improvement placed on the next Sprint Backlog
Review last retro's action(s) first at the start of every retro
Escalate a recurring complaint that is outside the team's control as an impediment rather than a retro action
Extend the retro timebox so there is more time to vent
Invite the manager so decisions get approved faster
questions.explanation: Groundhog Day is insight without follow-through; the fixes are one owned/dated action on the next Sprint Backlog, reviewing last time's action first, and escalating out-of-team-control issues as impediments. Extending the timebox just enables more venting, and a manager's presence typically reduces candor.

questions.question: Which practices are recommended to keep a hybrid event fair to remote participants? (Select all that apply.)
options: One person, one device, one cursor — everyone joins the shared digital board individually, even people in the same room
Default cameras on and round-robin by name with the order stated
Shorten timeboxes and add more breaks because remote attention decays faster
Let the in-room group settle details in side-conversations to move faster
Rely on reading the room's body language to gauge silence
questions.explanation: The cardinal sin is two-class participation; the fixes are one-person-one-device on a shared board, cameras-on with named round-robin, and shorter timeboxes with breaks. In-room side-channels create the very two-class split to avoid, and remote facilitation can't rely on body language you can't see.

questions.question: In SAFe 6.0 (current in 2026), which statement about the marquee cross-team planning event is correct?
options: It is Planning Interval (PI) Planning — 'Program Increment' was renamed 'Planning Interval' (PI acronym kept) — facilitated by the Release Train Engineer and closed with a fist-of-five confidence vote
It is Program Increment Planning, facilitated by the Scrum Master, with the Program Board as its central artifact and no confidence vote at the close
It is Planning Interval Planning, facilitated by the Product Owner, and the PI acronym was dropped along with the old name
It is PI Planning, facilitated by the Release Train Engineer, but closed by a consensus vote requiring unanimous agreement from every team
questions.explanation: SAFe 6.0 renamed Program Increment to Planning Interval while keeping the PI acronym; the RTE facilitates and it closes with a fist-of-five confidence vote (below-3 hands trigger a re-plan). The Program Board was also renamed the ART Planning Board, and the close is a fist-of-five gradient, not a unanimous consensus vote.
